APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a clearly NLP-centric problem (computational argumentation mining) using contemporary neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF, BLCC, LSTM-ER, dependency parsers) and standard AM benchmarks (Persuasive Essays dataset) and metrics, so it is largely within scope for a top NLP/ML conference. The evaluation compares appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP, multiple parser variants) and uses accepted F1/level-α matching, which matches community expectations; however, missing details such as hyperparameter reporting, code release, and some modelling choices (e.g., omission of seq2seq baselines) weaken reproducibility and broad appeal. Overall the work is well positioned for the venue but would benefit from fuller experimental disclosure to meet top-conference standards. | B(4): This work is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it proposes neural end-to-end formulations (BiLSTM-CRF, BLCC, adaptations of LSTM-ER), compares against standard dependency parsers and prior ILP baselines, and evaluates with established AM metrics on the Persuasive Essays corpus—yielding new SOTA claims. The paper speaks to the right audience by advancing modeling choices and multi-task setups relevant to argumentation mining, but its reliance on a single dataset (PE), lack of reported hyperparameters/code, and admitted unexplored framings (e.g., seq2seq) are scope and reproducibility concerns that stop short of a perfect fit.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a clearly NLP-centric problem (computational argumentation mining) using contemporary neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF, BLCC, LSTM-ER, dependency parsers) and standard AM benchmarks (Persuasive Essays dataset) and metrics, so it is largely within scope for a top NLP/ML conference. The evaluation compares appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP, multiple parser variants) and uses accepted F1/level-α matching, which matches community expectations; however, missing details such as hyperparameter reporting, code release, and some modelling choices (e.g., omission of seq2seq baselines) weaken reproducibility and broad appeal. Overall the work is well positioned for the venue but would benefit from fuller experimental disclosure to meet top-conference standards.

reviewer_b.justification: This work is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it proposes neural end-to-end formulations (BiLSTM-CRF, BLCC, adaptations of LSTM-ER), compares against standard dependency parsers and prior ILP baselines, and evaluates with established AM metrics on the Persuasive Essays corpus—yielding new SOTA claims. The paper speaks to the right audience by advancing modeling choices and multi-task setups relevant to argumentation mining, but its reliance on a single dataset (PE), lack of reported hyperparameters/code, and admitted unexplored framings (e.g., seq2seq) are scope and reproducibility concerns that stop short of a perfect fit.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper provides a broad empirical comparison (multiple neural architectures, several dependency parsers, and the ILP baseline) on the PE dataset and supports the main claims that simpler token-level BiLSTM taggers perform robustly and that dependency-parsing framing underperforms. However, important methodological details are missing for reproducibility (no hyperparameters, random seeds, or code reported) and there is little evidence of systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses to isolate which architectural choices drive gains. Related work appears well covered and some limitations/negative results (parsing framing, omitted seq2seq exploration) are acknowledged, but deeper failure analysis and reproducibility materials are lacking, so the experimental depth is adequate but incomplete. | B(3): The paper evaluates a wide range of neural and feature-based baselines on the PE corpus and tests multiple modeling framings (tagging, dependency parsing, multi-task tagging), which supports several of its claims. However, experiments are limited to a single dataset, no ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., hyperparameters or component contributions) are reported, and reproduction details (hyperparameters, code) are missing. Related work appears well covered, and some limitations (poor performance of parsing framing, encoder–decoder left for future work) are acknowledged, but the empirical evaluation lacks depth to fully validate generality and robustness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a broad empirical comparison (multiple neural architectures, several dependency parsers, and the ILP baseline) on the PE dataset and supports the main claims that simpler token-level BiLSTM taggers perform robustly and that dependency-parsing framing underperforms. However, important methodological details are missing for reproducibility (no hyperparameters, random seeds, or code reported) and there is little evidence of systematic ablation or sensitivity analyses to isolate which architectural choices drive gains. Related work appears well covered and some limitations/negative results (parsing framing, omitted seq2seq exploration) are acknowledged, but deeper failure analysis and reproducibility materials are lacking, so the experimental depth is adequate but incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates a wide range of neural and feature-based baselines on the PE corpus and tests multiple modeling framings (tagging, dependency parsing, multi-task tagging), which supports several of its claims. However, experiments are limited to a single dataset, no ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., hyperparameters or component contributions) are reported, and reproduction details (hyperparameters, code) are missing. Related work appears well covered, and some limitations (poor performance of parsing framing, encoder–decoder left for future work) are acknowledged, but the empirical evaluation lacks depth to fully validate generality and robustness.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles an important applied problem (end-to-end computational argumentation mining) and reports new state-of-the-art results on the widely used Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset, which will interest researchers working on AM. Its finding that simpler token-level BiLSTM tagging (including BiLSTM-CRF / BLCC) and multi-task setups outperform token-based dependency-parsing framings is a practical, actionable result that can change modeling choices in the subfield. However, the impact is moderate rather than transformative: evaluation is limited to a single relatively small dataset (402 essays), training details and code are not reported in the summary, and broader generalization beyond this dataset and to other framing approaches (e.g., seq2seq) remains untested. Thus the contribution is useful and likely to be built upon within the AM community but is unlikely to fundamentally shift NLP more broadly. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem—end-to-end computational argumentation mining—and demonstrates practical consequences by achieving new state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays corpus while showing that token-based dependency parsing formulations underperform. Its empirical finding that relatively simple, local BiLSTM-CRF/BLCC taggers (and multi-task learning variants) are robust and can capture long-range dependencies challenges a common assumption that more complex structured models or ILP are necessary, which is likely to change experimental priorities in the field. The work opens clear follow-ups (neural end-to-end AM, multi-task designs, comparisons to seq2seq) and is likely to be built upon by researchers seeking practical, scalable AM systems, giving it strong but not fully transformative impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important applied problem (end-to-end computational argumentation mining) and reports new state-of-the-art results on the widely used Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset, which will interest researchers working on AM. Its finding that simpler token-level BiLSTM tagging (including BiLSTM-CRF / BLCC) and multi-task setups outperform token-based dependency-parsing framings is a practical, actionable result that can change modeling choices in the subfield. However, the impact is moderate rather than transformative: evaluation is limited to a single relatively small dataset (402 essays), training details and code are not reported in the summary, and broader generalization beyond this dataset and to other framing approaches (e.g., seq2seq) remains untested. Thus the contribution is useful and likely to be built upon within the AM community but is unlikely to fundamentally shift NLP more broadly.

Its empirical finding that relatively simple, local BiLSTM-CRF/BLCC taggers (and multi-task learning variants) are robust and can capture long-range dependencies challenges a common assumption that more complex structured models or ILP are necessary, which is likely to change experimental priorities in the field. The work opens clear follow-ups (neural end-to-end AM, multi-task designs, comparisons to seq2seq) and is likely to be built upon by researchers seeking practical, scalable AM systems, giving it strong but not fully transformative impact.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper makes clear claims and supports them with experiments comparing a range of reasonable baselines (feature-based ILP, several dependency parsers, BiLSTM-CRF/BLCC taggers, LSTM-ER) on the PE dataset and reports component/relation F1 using established matching criteria, which substantiates the main findings (SOTA on PE, parsing performs poorly, tagging and multi-task models work well). However, methodological gaps remain: hyperparameters, training details, random seeds, and code availability are not reported, and no statistical significance testing is described, which limits reproducibility and confidence in small performance differences. There are no mathematical proofs to assess, and while the arguments are generally consistent with the results, stronger diagnostic analyses (ablation studies, error analysis) and full experimental details are needed to fully validate all claims. | B(4): The paper's empirical claims are generally supported: experiments on the PE dataset compare neural taggers (BiLSTM-CRF, BLCC), adapted LSTM-ER, and multiple dependency parsers and show improved end-to-end results and poorer performance for the dependency-parsing framing. Methodological coverage of baselines is strong (feature-based ILP, MST/Mate, neural parsers). However, the summary indicates missing experimental details (no hyperparameter reporting or statistical significance testing) and no code release noted, which are minor but important omissions for reproducibility and assessing robustness; there are no formal mathematical derivations to check. Overall the technical evidence supports the conclusions but reproducibility gaps prevent a perfect score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes clear claims and supports them with experiments comparing a range of reasonable baselines (feature-based ILP, several dependency parsers, BiLSTM-CRF/BLCC taggers, LSTM-ER) on the PE dataset and reports component/relation F1 using established matching criteria, which substantiates the main findings (SOTA on PE, parsing performs poorly, tagging and multi-task models work well). However, methodological gaps remain: hyperparameters, training details, random seeds, and code availability are not reported, and no statistical significance testing is described, which limits reproducibility and confidence in small performance differences. There are no mathematical proofs to assess, and while the arguments are generally consistent with the results, stronger diagnostic analyses (ablation studies, error analysis) and full experimental details are needed to fully validate all claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's empirical claims are generally supported: experiments on the PE dataset compare neural taggers (BiLSTM-CRF, BLCC), adapted LSTM-ER, and multiple dependency parsers and show improved end-to-end results and poorer performance for the dependency-parsing framing. Methodological coverage of baselines is strong (feature-based ILP, MST/Mate, neural parsers). However, the summary indicates missing experimental details (no hyperparameter reporting or statistical significance testing) and no code release noted, which are minor but important omissions for reproducibility and assessing robustness; there are no formal mathematical derivations to check. Overall the technical evidence supports the conclusions but reproducibility gaps prevent a perfect score.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early (claims about state-of-the-art on the PE dataset, comparisons of token-based parsing vs. tagging, and multi-task benefits). Notation and methodological framing appear consistent and the related-work coverage is thorough, which aids comprehension. Figures/tables/examples are described as effective and the writing quality is adequate, but a few presentation omissions (e.g., no mention of hyperparameter reporting or code availability) reduce reproducibility and slightly weaken clarity for readers trying to replicate experiments. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (introduction of end-to-end neural framing, comparison of parsing vs. tagging, and multi-task tagging gains). Notation and method descriptions appear consistent and related work coverage is thorough, and figures/tables/examples are informative enough to support the experiments on the PE dataset. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and some implementation details (e.g., exact matching levels and span-handling) could be clearer for reproducibility. From a computational-argumentation perspective the writing is adequate but would benefit from a brief appendix with training details to reach exceptional clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated early (claims about state-of-the-art on the PE dataset, comparisons of token-based parsing vs. tagging, and multi-task benefits). Notation and methodological framing appear consistent and the related-work coverage is thorough, which aids comprehension. Figures/tables/examples are described as effective and the writing quality is adequate, but a few presentation omissions (e.g., no mention of hyperparameter reporting or code availability) reduce reproducibility and slightly weaken clarity for readers trying to replicate experiments.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (introduction of end-to-end neural framing, comparison of parsing vs. tagging, and multi-task tagging gains). Notation and method descriptions appear consistent and related work coverage is thorough, and figures/tables/examples are informative enough to support the experiments on the PE dataset. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters and code availability are not reported, and some implementation details (e.g., exact matching levels and span-handling) could be clearer for reproducibility. From a computational-argumentation perspective the writing is adequate but would benefit from a brief appendix with training details to reach exceptional clarity.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper does not introduce fundamentally new algorithms; it adapts established neural sequence-tagging and parsing architectures (BiLSTM-CRF/BLCC, LSTM-ER, dependency parsers) to the Persuasive Essays argumentation mining task and compares them empirically. Its main novelty is methodological/application-level: framing AM as token-based sequence tagging and token-based dependency parsing and demonstrating that simpler BiLSTM-based taggers and multi-task learning outperform parsing and ILP baselines on the PE dataset. This is a useful, empirically-driven contribution but largely incremental since the core techniques are existing models and the claimed improvements are empirical rather than conceptual innovations. | B(3): From the summary, the paper applies end-to-end neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF, BLCC, adaptations of LSTM-ER) and a token-based dependency parsing framing to the established Persuasive Essays dataset and shows that simpler tagging and multi-task setups work well. While the comprehensive empirical comparison (against ILP baselines and multiple parsers) and the negative result for dependency-parsing framing are useful, the core methods are largely standard neural sequence-labeling and parsing techniques rather than fundamentally new algorithms or formulations. Thus the contribution is a solid, informative empirical study with moderate novelty rather than a highly original methodological advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does not introduce fundamentally new algorithms; it adapts established neural sequence-tagging and parsing architectures (BiLSTM-CRF/BLCC, LSTM-ER, dependency parsers) to the Persuasive Essays argumentation mining task and compares them empirically. Its main novelty is methodological/application-level: framing AM as token-based sequence tagging and token-based dependency parsing and demonstrating that simpler BiLSTM-based taggers and multi-task learning outperform parsing and ILP baselines on the PE dataset. This is a useful, empirically-driven contribution but largely incremental since the core techniques are existing models and the claimed improvements are empirical rather than conceptual innovations.

reviewer_b.justification: From the summary, the paper applies end-to-end neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF, BLCC, adaptations of LSTM-ER) and a token-based dependency parsing framing to the established Persuasive Essays dataset and shows that simpler tagging and multi-task setups work well. While the comprehensive empirical comparison (against ILP baselines and multiple parsers) and the negative result for dependency-parsing framing are useful, the core methods are largely standard neural sequence-labeling and parsing techniques rather than fundamentally new algorithms or formulations. Thus the contribution is a solid, informative empirical study with moderate novelty rather than a highly original methodological advance.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against a strong set of baselines (the Stab & Gurevych ILP model on the same PE dataset, several classical and neural dependency parsers, and neural sequence taggers such as BLCC and LSTM-ER) and uses the established metrics from Persing & Ng (2016), which supports largely fair comparisons. The authors explicitly acknowledge when parsing formulations underperform and present analyses contrasting token-based tagging vs. parsing and multitask tagging benefits. However, the paper omits important reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code availability) that could affect fairness of comparisons, and it only briefly notes but does not explore other plausible framings (e.g., seq2seq), so I deduct one point for these minor but meaningful omissions. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of relevant baselines including the feature-based ILP system from Stab and Gurevych (2016), dependency parsers (MST, Mate), recent neural parsers (Dyer; Kiperwasser & Goldberg), strong tagging models (STagBLCC), and LSTM-ER, all evaluated on the same Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset with component/relation F1 metrics used in prior work. Comparisons appear fair and use the established evaluation protocol (Persing & Ng-style matching), and the authors acknowledge when parsing formulations underperform; however, they omit some reasonable alternative end-to-end formulations (e.g., sequence-to-sequence/encoder-decoder baselines) and do not report hyperparameter/code details, which are minor but notable gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against a strong set of baselines (the Stab & Gurevych ILP model on the same PE dataset, several classical and neural dependency parsers, and neural sequence taggers such as BLCC and LSTM-ER) and uses the established metrics from Persing & Ng (2016), which supports largely fair comparisons. The authors explicitly acknowledge when parsing formulations underperform and present analyses contrasting token-based tagging vs. parsing and multitask tagging benefits. However, the paper omits important reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code availability) that could affect fairness of comparisons, and it only briefly notes but does not explore other plausible framings (e.g., seq2seq), so I deduct one point for these minor but meaningful omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of relevant baselines including the feature-based ILP system from Stab and Gurevych (2016), dependency parsers (MST, Mate), recent neural parsers (Dyer; Kiperwasser & Goldberg), strong tagging models (STagBLCC), and LSTM-ER, all evaluated on the same Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset with component/relation F1 metrics used in prior work. Comparisons appear fair and use the established evaluation protocol (Persing & Ng-style matching), and the authors acknowledge when parsing formulations underperform; however, they omit some reasonable alternative end-to-end formulations (e.g., sequence-to-sequence/encoder-decoder baselines) and do not report hyperparameter/code details, which are minor but notable gaps.

main_claims: The proposed neural end-to-end approach yields new state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset from Stab and Gurevych (2016).
Framing argumentation mining as token-based dependency parsing leads to subpar performance compared to other approaches.
Less complex, local tagging models based on BiLSTMs (including BiLSTM-CRF and BLCC variants) perform robustly across classification scenarios and can capture long-range dependencies.
Jointly learning natural subtasks in a multi-task learning setup improves performance for argumentation mining.
_summary.methodology: They frame computational argumentation mining as token-based dependency parsing and token-based sequence tagging, including multi-task (tagging) setups, and evaluate neural architectures such as BiLSTM-CRF, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN (BLCC), and adaptations of LSTM-ER, comparing also to dependency parsers and a feature-based ILP system.
datasets: Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset from Stab and Gurevych (2016) (402 essays, 80 reserved for testing)
baselines: Feature-based ILP model from Stab and Gurevych (2016)
Dependency parsers: MST (McDonald et al., 2005), Mate (Bohnet and Nivre, 2012)
Neural parsers: Dyer et al. (2015) LSTM-Parser, Kiperwasser and Goldberg (2016)
STagBLCC (BLCC tagger from Ma and Hovy, 2016)
metrics: Component and relation F1 (computed from TP, FP, FN) as suggested in Persing and Ng (2016); uses level-α matching (e.g., 100% exact span match, 50% approximate match)
limitations: Framing AM as dependency parsing yields subpar performance.
Encoder-decoder framing (sequence-to-sequence) was not considered here and its suitability for end-to-end AM remains scope for future work.
Hand-crafted features pose a problem for AM due to reliance on underlying argumentation theory (stated as a challenge).

reviewer_a.name: Methodological Systems Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with extensive experience building and evaluating neural NLP systems, reproducible benchmarking, and deploying models in research and production settings. Has co-authored papers on experimental best practices, ablation studies, and evaluation protocols in sequence labeling and parsing tasks.
traits: insists on clear, reproducible experimental setup (data splits, preprocessing, hyperparameters, random seeds, and code availability)
focuses on baseline selection, proper statistical comparison, and whether ablation studies support claimed contributions
looks for thorough error analysis and diagnostic experiments that explain when models fail
values reporting of training details (optimization, early stopping, compute) and sensitivity to hyperparameters
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper by checking whether the experiments and analyses convincingly support the claims, and requests missing results or experiments needed to validate conclusions.

reviewer_b.name: Computational Argumentation Scholar
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in computational argumentation and discourse analysis, with a track record of work on argument mining datasets, annotation schemas, and applied uses in education, law, and online discourse. Frequently reviews papers on task formulations and cross-dataset generalization in argument mining.
traits: examines the novelty and fit of task formulations relative to prior corpora and annotation schemes
assesses whether model choices and evaluation metrics reflect realistic use-cases and downstream impact
notices connections to related work (ILP, joint models, multi-task learning) and whether contributions advance the field beyond incremental gains
values discussion of generalizability across datasets and practical implications for argumentation research and applications
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Places the work in the broader argumentation-mining literature, judging novelty, relevance, and potential impact while highlighting missing comparisons or real-world considerations.
